doc.title: Two-Sided Repair Marketplace — market entry strategy

pushing_hardest_on: The supply side of a two-sided marketplace is never addressed
Price is treated as a moat when it is a wedge a funded incumbent copies
No stage is stated — the plan assumes product-market fit and jumps to geo-scaling

axes.reason: The demand segment is specific, but it is asserted with no sizing or evidence, and the supply side (the professionals) — the other half of the marketplace — is entirely absent.
axes.quote: Homeowners aged thirty to fifty in mid-size metros who are comfortable booking services on their phone
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Sizing and demand evidence for the homeowner segment, plus a full profile of the supply side: who the professionals are and what makes them join and stay.

axes.reason: A market-entry strategy doc — the product solution hypothesis is carried by the Strategy axis, not a separate PRD-style solution.
A growth sequence, not a scope — no explicit IN / OUT / CUT and no statement of what is deliberately deferred.
axes.quote: Launch in one metro, subsidize the first thousand bookings to seed demand, and grow city by city.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: IN / OUT / CUT for the first metro: which job categories, which neighborhoods, what is explicitly not attempted in v1.

axes.reason: CAC is named only as a spending guardrail; there is no outcome metric for the strategy — no target take-rate, liquidity, retention, baseline, or kill threshold.
axes.quote: We will watch CAC closely and slow spending if it runs high.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A liquidity outcome (e.g. % of requests filled within X hours) with baseline, target, timeframe, and a counter-metric on quality.

axes.reason: Price leadership is a wedge, not a moat — a 10% take rate is trivially matched by a funded incumbent, and there is no willingness-to-pay evidence beyond 'cheaper'.
axes.quote: Our take rate of ten percent undercuts the incumbents' fifteen to twenty percent
axes.what_id_want_to_see: Evidence either side will pay a sustainable rate, and what durable position the low take rate buys time to build.

axes.reason: Asserts a scale flywheel but names no mechanism for why scale durably lowers a marketplace's prices; 'cheaper and simpler' is copyable, not a structural advantage.
axes.quote: As we grow, our scale lets us push prices down further, which brings in more customers.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A named moat — supply-side switching costs, liquidity/network effects, or proprietary trust/vetting data — and why it compounds.

axes.reason: The demand-seeding motion is concrete, but supply-side acquisition — the harder half of marketplace cold-start — is unaddressed.
axes.quote: subsidize the first thousand bookings to seed demand, and grow city by city
axes.what_id_want_to_see: How the first professionals are recruited and vetted before demand subsidies arrive, so the first bookings have anyone to fulfill them.

axes.reason: The lifecycle stage is never stated; the plan assumes product-market fit and proceeds straight to city-by-city scaling.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: An explicit stage (this is pre-PMF market entry) and the evidence — fill rate, repeat rate — that would justify geo-scaling.
n/a — no model-backed behavior to govern.

axes.reason: A single demand-side risk (CAC) with no pre-mortem; the existential marketplace risks — supply liquidity, vetting/quality failure, and off-platform leakage — are all missing.
axes.quote: The main risk is that customer acquisition is more expensive than planned.
axes.what_id_want_to_see: A pre-mortem covering supply liquidity collapse, a vetting failure that injures trust, and customers and pros booking off-platform after the first match.

weakest_claims.claim: The marketplace can be planned from the demand side alone.
weakest_claims.quote: subsidize the first thousand bookings to seed demand, and grow city by city
followups: Who supplies the vetted professionals, and how are they acquired and retained before demand arrives?
Who pays for 'vetted', and what does a vetting failure cost the brand?

weakest_claims.claim: Scale creates a durable price advantage.
weakest_claims.quote: As we grow, our scale lets us push prices down further, which brings in more customers.
followups: What structurally stops a funded incumbent from matching a 10% take rate next quarter?
Does lower price compound into a moat, or just compress everyone's margins?

weakest_claims.claim: Lowest price plus simplest app is the winning differentiation.
weakest_claims.quote: We are cheaper and simpler than anyone else in the market.
followups: Both are copyable within a quarter — what is left when an incumbent matches them?
Is 'simpler' evidenced by any usability benchmark, or asserted?

strengths: A clear, concrete demand-seeding motion (subsidize first bookings, then geo-expand)
An honest, specific target demand segment
must_dos: Model the supply side — acquisition, vetting cost, and liquidity per metro
Name a durable advantage beyond price and simplicity
State the stage and the PMF evidence the geo-scaling assumes

limits: Unit economics (CAC, LTV, contribution margin) are not in this excerpt — they may live in a financial model
